clusters.title: Parents’ stress may be quietly driving childhood obesity, Yale study finds
clusters.summary: A Yale study found that lowering parent stress can help protect young children from obesity. When parents practiced mindfulness and stress-management skills, their kids showed healthier eating patterns and avoided the weight gain seen in families that only focused on diet and exercise.

items.title: Parents’ stress may be quietly driving childhood obesity, Yale study finds
items.description: A Yale study found that lowering parent stress can help protect young children from obesity. When parents practiced mindfulness and stress-management skills, their kids showed healthier eating patterns and avoided the weight gain seen in families that only focused on diet and exercise.

clusters.title: Why tipping keeps rising and may not improve service
clusters.summary: Why do we tip—even when we know we’ll never see the server again? New research suggests it’s not just about rewarding good service, but about social pressure. Some people tip out of genuine appreciation, while others simply follow the norm.

items.title: Why tipping keeps rising and may not improve service
items.description: Why do we tip—even when we know we’ll never see the server again? New research suggests it’s not just about rewarding good service, but about social pressure. Some people tip out of genuine appreciation, while others simply follow the norm. But here’s the twist: those who truly value great service tend to tip more than average, and everyone else adjusts upward to match them.

clusters.title: Neurons receive precisely tailored teaching signals as we learn, study suggests
clusters.summary: When we learn a new skill, the brain has to decide—cell by cell—what to change. New research from MIT suggests it can do that with surprising precision, sending targeted feedback to individual neurons so each one can adjust its activity in the right direction.

items.title: Neurons receive precisely tailored teaching signals as we learn, study suggests
items.description: When we learn a new skill, the brain has to decide—cell by cell—what to change. New research from MIT suggests it can do that with surprising precision, sending targeted feedback to individual neurons so each one can adjust its activity in the right direction.

clusters.title: Families Scramble To Pay Five-Figure Bills as Clock Ticks on Promised Preauthorization Reforms
clusters.summary: Last summer, the Trump administration announced a voluntary pledge by health insurers to reform prior authorization, which often requires patients or their doctors to seek preapproval from insurers before proceeding with medical care. Patient advocates and medical providers remain skeptical.

items.title: Families Scramble To Pay Five-Figure Bills as Clock Ticks on Promised Preauthorization Reforms
items.description: Last summer, the Trump administration announced a voluntary pledge by health insurers to reform prior authorization, which often requires patients or their doctors to seek preapproval from insurers before proceeding with medical care. Patient advocates and medical providers remain skeptical.

clusters.title: People consistently overestimate the social backlash of changing their political beliefs, new psychology research shows
clusters.summary: A recent study in the Journal of Personality and Social Psychology suggests people consistently overestimate how much their political peers will judge them for changing their minds. This exaggerated fear tends to stifle open public discourse through self-censorship.

items.title: People consistently overestimate the social backlash of changing their political beliefs, new psychology research shows
items.description: A recent study in the Journal of Personality and Social Psychology suggests people consistently overestimate how much their political peers will judge them for changing their minds. This exaggerated fear tends to stifle open public discourse through self-censorship.

clusters.title: The extreme male brain theory of autism applies more strongly to females
clusters.summary: A comprehensive new study suggests that females require a much larger biological and cognitive shift to develop autism than males do. The findings help explain why the condition is diagnosed significantly more often in boys.

items.title: The extreme male brain theory of autism applies more strongly to females
items.description: A comprehensive new study suggests that females require a much larger biological and cognitive shift to develop autism than males do. The findings help explain why the condition is diagnosed significantly more often in boys.

clusters.title: Veterans are no more likely than the general public to support political violence
clusters.summary: Research published in Injury Epidemiology suggests widespread extremist attitudes among military veterans are rare. While veterans are not broadly prone to political violence, the study notes that those who do radicalize remain highly dangerous.

items.title: Veterans are no more likely than the general public to support political violence
items.description: Research published in Injury Epidemiology suggests widespread extremist attitudes among military veterans are rare. While veterans are not broadly prone to political violence, the study notes that those who do radicalize remain highly dangerous.

clusters.title: The more you fear aging, the faster your body may age
clusters.summary: Worrying about getting older—especially fearing future health problems—may actually speed up aging at the cellular level, according to new research from NYU. In a study of more than 700 women, those who felt more anxious about aging showed signs of faster biological aging in their blood, measured using cutting-edge “epigenetic clocks.” Fears about declining health had the strongest link, while concerns about beauty or fertility didn’t appear to have the same biological impact.

items.title: The more you fear aging, the faster your body may age
items.description: Worrying about getting older—especially fearing future health problems—may actually speed up aging at the cellular level, according to new research from NYU. In a study of more than 700 women, those who felt more anxious about aging showed signs of faster biological aging in their blood, measured using cutting-edge “epigenetic clocks.” Fears about declining health had the strongest link, while concerns about beauty or fertility didn’t appear to have the same biological impact.

clusters.title: Depression may start with an energy problem in brain cells
clusters.summary: Researchers have discovered a surprising change in how cells produce energy in people with depression. Brain and blood cells in young adults with major depressive disorder produced more energy molecules at rest but had trouble increasing energy production when needed. Scientists believe this imbalance may contribute to symptoms such as fatigue and low motivation.

items.title: Depression may start with an energy problem in brain cells
items.description: Researchers have discovered a surprising change in how cells produce energy in people with depression. Brain and blood cells in young adults with major depressive disorder produced more energy molecules at rest but had trouble increasing energy production when needed. Scientists believe this imbalance may contribute to symptoms such as fatigue and low motivation. The finding could help pave the way for earlier diagnosis and more personalized treatments.

clusters.title: People with social anxiety are less likely to experience a post-sex emotional glow
clusters.summary: New research reveals that while single adults with social anxiety engage in sex at typical rates, these interactions are not followed by the usual "morning-after glow" of positive emotions seen in their peers.

items.title: People with social anxiety are less likely to experience a post-sex emotional glow
items.description: New research reveals that while single adults with social anxiety engage in sex at typical rates, these interactions are not followed by the usual "morning-after glow" of positive emotions seen in their peers.

clusters.title: New drug cuts seizures by up to 91% in children with rare epilepsy
clusters.summary: A new experimental drug is showing remarkable promise for children with Dravet syndrome, a severe genetic form of epilepsy. In clinical trials, the treatment zorevunersen cut seizures by as much as 91% while also improving quality of life for many patients. The therapy works by boosting the function of a key gene involved in nerve cell signaling.

items.title: New drug cuts seizures by up to 91% in children with rare epilepsy
items.description: A new experimental drug is showing remarkable promise for children with Dravet syndrome, a severe genetic form of epilepsy. In clinical trials, the treatment zorevunersen cut seizures by as much as 91% while also improving quality of life for many patients. The therapy works by boosting the function of a key gene involved in nerve cell signaling. Encouraging results have led researchers to launch a larger Phase 3 trial.

clusters.title: Scientists discover the brain protein that drives cocaine relapse
clusters.summary: Cocaine addiction isn’t simply a failure of willpower — it’s the result of lasting biological changes in the brain. Researchers at Michigan State University discovered that repeated cocaine use rewires communication between the brain’s reward system and the hippocampus, the region responsible for memory. A protein called DeltaFosB builds up with continued drug use and acts like a genetic switch, altering how neurons function and strengthening the brain’s drive to seek cocaine.

items.title: Scientists discover the brain protein that drives cocaine relapse
items.description: Cocaine addiction isn’t simply a failure of willpower — it’s the result of lasting biological changes in the brain. Researchers at Michigan State University discovered that repeated cocaine use rewires communication between the brain’s reward system and the hippocampus, the region responsible for memory. A protein called DeltaFosB builds up with continued drug use and acts like a genetic switch, altering how neurons function and strengthening the brain’s drive to seek cocaine.

clusters.title: Gut bacteria that make serotonin may hold the key to IBS
clusters.summary: Researchers have identified two gut bacteria that can produce serotonin, a key chemical that regulates bowel movements. In experiments with mice lacking serotonin, the microbes boosted serotonin levels, increased nerve cells in the colon, and normalized intestinal movement. The study also found that people with IBS have lower levels of one of these bacteria.

items.title: Gut bacteria that make serotonin may hold the key to IBS
items.description: Researchers have identified two gut bacteria that can produce serotonin, a key chemical that regulates bowel movements. In experiments with mice lacking serotonin, the microbes boosted serotonin levels, increased nerve cells in the colon, and normalized intestinal movement. The study also found that people with IBS have lower levels of one of these bacteria. The discovery suggests gut microbes could become a powerful new target for treating digestive disorders.

clusters.title: This ancient sea creature may already have had a brain
clusters.summary: Advanced 3D reconstructions of the comb jelly’s aboral organ reveal a sensory system far more complex than scientists expected. The organ contains a wide variety of specialized cells and is closely linked to the animal’s nerve network, allowing it to coordinate behavior and orientation in the water. Researchers say it may function as a primitive brain-like center.

items.title: This ancient sea creature may already have had a brain
items.description: Advanced 3D reconstructions of the comb jelly’s aboral organ reveal a sensory system far more complex than scientists expected. The organ contains a wide variety of specialized cells and is closely linked to the animal’s nerve network, allowing it to coordinate behavior and orientation in the water. Researchers say it may function as a primitive brain-like center. The discovery suggests that centralized nervous systems might have evolved independently in different animal lineages.

clusters.title: Researchers identify personality traits that predict alcohol relapse after treatment
clusters.summary: A person's basic personality traits might help predict if they will start drinking again after alcohol withdrawal. A new study shows that people with a strong desire for new experiences are more likely to relapse within three months.

items.title: Researchers identify personality traits that predict alcohol relapse after treatment
items.description: A person's basic personality traits might help predict if they will start drinking again after alcohol withdrawal. A new study shows that people with a strong desire for new experiences are more likely to relapse within three months.

clusters.title: Boosting a key brain protein could help treat Rett syndrome
clusters.summary: Researchers have discovered a new way to increase a key brain protein damaged in Rett syndrome, a rare genetic disorder that affects thousands of children worldwide. Early studies in mice and patient-derived cells show the approach can restore normal brain cell function, raising hopes for future therapies.

items.title: Boosting a key brain protein could help treat Rett syndrome
items.description: Researchers have discovered a new way to increase a key brain protein damaged in Rett syndrome, a rare genetic disorder that affects thousands of children worldwide. Early studies in mice and patient-derived cells show the approach can restore normal brain cell function, raising hopes for future therapies.

clusters.title: Telehealth visits may help breast cancer patients stay on treatment
clusters.summary: Staying in touch with doctors online could help breast cancer survivors keep up their cancer treatment, according to a recent study from the University of Georgia published in the Journal of Cancer Survivorship.

items.title: Telehealth visits may help breast cancer patients stay on treatment
items.description: Staying in touch with doctors online could help breast cancer survivors keep up their cancer treatment, according to a recent study from the University of Georgia published in the Journal of Cancer Survivorship.

clusters.title: Microplastics may be quietly damaging your brain and fueling Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s
clusters.summary: Tiny plastic particles may be quietly threatening brain health. New research suggests microplastics—now widely found in food, water, and even household dust—could trigger inflammation and damage in the brain through multiple biological pathways. Scientists estimate adults may consume about 250 grams of these particles each year, and some can accumulate in organs including the brain.

items.title: Microplastics may be quietly damaging your brain and fueling Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s

New research suggests microplastics—now widely found in food, water, and even household dust—could trigger inflammation and damage in the brain through multiple biological pathways. Scientists estimate adults may consume about 250 grams of these particles each year, and some can accumulate in organs including the brain.

clusters.title: Your personality and upbringing predict if you will lean toward science or faith
clusters.summary: A new psychological study reveals how specific childhood experiences and inherent personality traits predict whether adults will use scientific evidence or religious faith to make sense of the world around them.

items.title: Your personality and upbringing predict if you will lean toward science or faith
items.description: A new psychological study reveals how specific childhood experiences and inherent personality traits predict whether adults will use scientific evidence or religious faith to make sense of the world around them.

clusters.title: Common pneumonia bacterium may fuel Alzheimer’s disease
clusters.summary: A common bacterium best known for causing pneumonia and sinus infections may also play a surprising role in Alzheimer’s disease. Researchers found that Chlamydia pneumoniae can invade the retina and brain, where it sparks inflammation, nerve cell death, and the buildup of amyloid-beta—the hallmark protein linked to Alzheimer’s. Higher levels of the bacterium were found in people with Alzheimer’s, especially those carrying the high-risk APOE4 gene, and were tied to more severe cognitive decline.
